response:
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) and UDP (User Datagram Protocol) are the two primary transport layer protocols in the Internet Protocol (IP) suite. Both rely on 16-bit port numbers to route data to specific applications running on the same host, but they are built for fundamentally different priorities: TCP prioritizes data integrity, while UDP prioritizes low latency and minimal overhead. Below is a detailed side-by-side comparison:

| Category | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) |
|----------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Core Foundation | Connection-oriented: Requires a pre-established logical link between sender and receiver before any data is transmitted | Connectionless: No prior link is required; sender transmits independent data packets (datagrams) to the receiver as they are generated |
| Connection Lifecycle Management | Uses a 3-way handshake to open connections, and a 4-way handshake to close them; connection state is tracked for the full session | No connection setup or teardown; no overhead spent managing a session lifecycle |
| Statefulness | Stateful: Both endpoints maintain shared connection state (sequence numbers, window sizes, etc.) for the session’s duration | Stateless: No shared connection state; each datagram is processed entirely independently |
| Reliability Guarantees | Highly reliable: Uses acknowledgments (ACKs) to confirm delivery; automatically retransmits lost, unacknowledged, or corrupted packets | Best-effort only: No mechanism to confirm delivery or retransmit lost data, only an optional basic checksum to detect corruption |
| In-order Delivery | Guaranteed: Sequence numbers assigned to all packets let the receiver reconstruct data in the exact order it was sent | No guarantees: Datagrams may arrive out of order, and are passed to the application immediately with no reordering |
| Duplicate Packet Prevention | Built-in: Sequence numbers allow receivers to detect and discard duplicate packets | No prevention: Duplicate datagrams are passed directly to the application if they arrive |
| Flow & Congestion Control | Full support: Uses sliding window flow control to avoid overwhelming the receiver, plus congestion control algorithms to prevent network overload | No built-in controls: Senders can transmit data as fast as their application allows, with no safeguards for receiver or network overload |
| Header Overhead | Variable 20–60 bytes (large overhead to support its extensive feature set) | Fixed
